diagrams.title: Time Complexity Growth Curves
diagrams.description: A coordinate plane showing growth rates of common time complexities: O(1), O(log n), O(n), O(n log n), O(n²), O(2^n). The x-axis represents input size n, the y-axis represents time/operations. Each curve demonstrates how algorithms scale as input grows, with O(1) being flat and O(2^n) skyrocketing.
diagrams.purpose: Visualizes how different algorithmic complexities grow, helping students understand why O(1) is preferable to O(n²) and the dramatic impact of exponential growth

diagrams.title: Big-O, Big-Ω, and Big-Θ Bounds Visualization
diagrams.description: Three side-by-side graphs demonstrating asymptotic bounds. For Big-O (upper bound): f(n) stays below c*g(n) after n₀. For Big-Ω (lower bound): f(n) stays above c*g(n) after n₀. For Big-Θ (tight bound): f(n) is sandwiched between c₁*g(n) and c₂*g(n) after n₀. Uses different colored regions to show the bounded areas.
elements: Big-O panel with f(n) curve below c*g(n)
Big-Ω panel with f(n) curve above c*g(n)
Big-Θ panel with f(n) curve between two bounds
c and n₀ markers on each graph
axis labels and title for each notation
diagrams.purpose: Clarifies the formal mathematical definitions of Big-O, Big-Omega, and Big-Theta by showing where the function lies relative to the bounding function

diagrams.title: Common Data Structure Operations Complexity Table
diagrams.description: A structured table showing time complexities (Best, Average, Worst cases) for fundamental data structure operations: Array access, Linked List search/insert, Stack push/pop, Queue enqueue/dequeue, Binary Search Tree operations, and Hash Table operations. Color-coded cells using heat map style (green for O(1), yellow for O(log n), orange for O(n), red for O(n²)).
elements: table with rows for data structures
columns for Access, Search, Insertion, Deletion
sub-columns for Best, Average, Worst cases
row headers (Array, Linked List, Stack, Queue, BST, Hash Table)
diagrams.purpose: Provides a quick reference for comparing algorithmic performance across different data structures, reinforcing when to use each structure based on operation requirements
